audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Danite land search, seizure of Micah's shrine and Levite, Laish destruction, and Dan shrine narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Dan's unsettled inheritance, five-person reconnaissance, Levite oracle, Laish's quiet and isolation, six-hundred-person armed migration, theft of shrine objects, clerical recruitment under armed pressure, Micah's protest, destruction of Laish, colonial renaming, Jonathan genealogy textual issue, and rival shrine chronology.

verses.restored: In those days there was no king in Israel. In those days tribe of Dan was seeking for itself an inheritance in which to dwell, because until that day an inheritance had not fallen to it among tribes of Israel.

Children of Dan sent from their clan five men from their whole number, men of strength, from Zorah and Eshtaol, to spy out land and search it. They said to them, Go, search land. They came to hill country of Ephraim, to house of Micah, and lodged there.
When they were near house of Micah, they recognized voice of young Levite. They turned aside there and said to him, Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place? What do you have here?
He said to them, Thus and thus Micah has done for me; he hired me, and I became his priest.
They said to him, Inquire, please, of Divine, so we may know whether our way upon which we are going will prosper.
The priest said to them, Go in peace. Before YHWH is your way upon which you are going.

The five men went and came to Laish. They saw people who were within it dwelling securely, according to manner of Sidonians, quiet and unsuspecting, with no one in the land humiliating or dispossessing them in any matter. They were far from Sidonians and had no dealings with other people.
They came to their brothers at Zorah and Eshtaol, and their brothers said to them, What do you report?
They said, Arise, and let us go up against them, because we have seen the land, and look, it is very good. Will you remain silent? Do not be slow to go, to enter, to possess the land.
When you go, you will come to an unsuspecting people and a broad land, because Divine has given it into your hand, a place where there is no lack of anything that is in the earth.
Six hundred men from clan of Dan set out from Zorah and Eshtaol, equipped with weapons of war.
They went up and camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah. Therefore they call that place Mahaneh-dan to this day; look, it is west of Kiriath-jearim.
They passed from there to hill country of Ephraim and came to house of Micah.

The five men who had gone to spy out land of Laish answered and said to their brothers, Do you know that in these houses are an ephod, teraphim, a carved image, and a cast image? Now know what you will do.
They turned aside there, came to house of young Levite, house of Micah, and asked him concerning peace.
The six hundred men from children of Dan, equipped with their weapons of war, stood at entrance of gate.

The five men who had gone to spy out land went up and entered there. They took carved image, ephod, teraphim, and cast image, while the priest stood at entrance of gate with the six hundred men equipped with weapons of war.
When these entered house of Micah and took carved image, ephod, teraphim, and cast image, the priest said to them, What are you doing?

They said to him, Be silent; place your hand upon your mouth and go with us, and be to us a father and a priest. Is it better for you to be priest to house of one man or to be priest to a tribe and clan in Israel?
Heart of the priest was glad. He took ephod, teraphim, and carved image and came into midst of people.
They turned and went, and put little ones, livestock, and valuable goods before them.
When they had gone some distance from house of Micah, men who were in houses near house of Micah were called together and overtook children of Dan.
They called to children of Dan. They turned their faces and said to Micah, What is wrong with you, that you have been called together?
He said, My gods that I made you have taken, and the priest, and you have gone away. What remains to me? How then can you say to me, What is wrong with you?
Children of Dan said to him, Do not let your voice be heard among us, lest men bitter of life strike you, and you gather up your life and life of your household.
Children of Dan went on their way. Micah saw that they were stronger than he, so he turned and went back to his house.
They took what Micah had made and the priest who had been his, and came upon Laish, upon a quiet and unsuspecting people. They struck them by edge of sword and burned city with fire.
There was no deliverer, because it was far from Sidon and they had no dealings with other people. It was in valley belonging to Beth-rehob. They built the city and dwelt in it.
They called name of city Dan, after name of Dan their father who was born to Israel; but Laish was name of city at first.
Children of Dan set up for themselves the carved image. Jonathan son of Gershom son of Manasseh/Moses, he and his sons were priests to tribe of Dan until day of captivity of land.
They set up for themselves carved image of Micah that he had made, all days that house of Divine was at Shiloh.

translation_notes: Dan's lack of secure territory is a real condition of displacement, but the narrative's response is not ethically innocent: scouts identify an isolated, peaceful population precisely because it can be attacked without rescue.

Verse 7 is textually difficult. Its central picture is clear: Laish is quiet, unsuspecting, distant from allies, and not expecting dispossession. Those conditions increase vulnerability; they do not create moral permission for conquest.

The Levite's oracle that their way is before YHWH is reported inside a compromised shrine economy. It must not be treated as Divine endorsement of theft, forced migration, colonial seizure, or massacre.

The six hundred armed men surround the priest while shrine objects are taken. His later gladness and career advancement occur within an armed takeover; the scene should not be simplified into fully free vocational choice.
The command to put a hand over the mouth is coercive silencing. Religious workers may not be pressured into changing allegiance by threats, prestige, or institutional scale.

Micah's claim, 'my gods that I made,' exposes his possessive religious logic, but Dan's superior force does not make their theft just. Critique of Micah's shrine cannot become permission for religious persecution or cultural plunder.

Laish is attacked as a quiet civilian settlement, burned, rebuilt, and renamed. This is colonial conquest and population destruction, not an uplifting model of claiming promise or finding one's place.

Jonathan's ancestry is textually sensitive. The Masoretic tradition visually inserts a suspended nun in 'Manasseh,' while many scholars understand an original 'Moses.' Later audit must preserve the witness history without anti-Moses polemic.

The Dan shrine is contrasted chronologically with the house of Divine at Shiloh. The text preserves a rivalry of sanctuaries; it must not be weaponized into antisemitism, supersessionism, or attacks on present-day worship communities.
safety_note: Maximum anti-colonial and anti-xenophobia safeguards: Laish's isolation, peace, wealth, or lack of military alliance never makes its people disposable. Do not glamorize armed reconnaissance, seizure of sacred objects, coerced clergy, civilian massacre, city burning, territorial replacement, or renaming conquered land. Do not turn this chapter into permission for modern ethnic cleansing, settler violence, religious theft, or claims that prosperity proves Divine authorization.
